1: Moses's first objection at the burning bush was about YHWH's trustworthiness (Exodus 3:11); this second is about Israel's. “What if they will not believe me?” — the Hebrew lo ya'aminu li — anticipates the prophetic problem of credibility. Deuteronomy 18:21-22 would later address this: signs and accurate prediction authenticate the prophet. The three signs YHWH provides here are exactly that: proofs of commission (Hebrew ot, sign), not mere displays of power. The pattern reflects ancient treaty and embassy culture: a messenger needed to carry the authenticating mark of the sender.
2: The staff (matteh) was the ordinary tool of a shepherd — a crook or rod used for guiding flocks, leaning on, and defending against predators. YHWH takes the mundane object Moses already holds and makes it the instrument of divine power. The same principle recurs throughout Scripture: Moses's staff, Gideon's torches and clay jars, David's sling. The ordinary becomes extraordinary when invested with divine commission. This staff is later called “the staff of God” (v20) and “the staff of YHWH” (Exodus 17:9) — the same object, renamed by its use.
3: The staff becomes a nachash (serpent) when thrown down. In Egyptian royal iconography the serpent was preeminent: the uraeus (cobra) was the emblem of the Pharaoh's crown, symbolizing his divine protection and kingship. When Aaron later repeats this sign before Pharaoh (Exodus 7:10-12), his serpent swallows the Egyptian magicians' serpents — a symbolic declaration that YHWH's authority swallows Egypt's royal power. The serpent motif also echoes Gen 3: the same creature that was the instrument of the Fall is here subject to YHWH's deliverer.
4: YHWH commands Moses to grab the serpent “by the tail” (biznavav) — the dangerous end. Anyone handling a serpent safely would grab it behind the head; grabbing by the tail requires either foolishness or divine protection. The act of obedient faith reverses the serpent's threat. The instant restoration — “it became a staff in his hand” — demonstrates divine control over the sign's operation. The sign is not magic that Moses performs but divine power he transmits by obedient action.
5: “So that they may believe that YHWH, the God of their fathers — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob — has appeared to you.” The signs serve epistemic faith (he'emin), not mere wonder. The patriarchal triad (Abraham/Isaac/Jacob) grounds the mission in covenant history — YHWH is not a new deity but the covenant God of the ancestors. This is the same divine self-identification given at the burning bush (Exodus 3:6) and cited by Jesus in his argument for the resurrection (Matthew 22:32).
6: The leprous hand sign (tzara'at) — the hand put inside the cloak and withdrawn white as snow. Tzara'at (usually translated “leprosy” but encompassing various skin conditions) was the supreme ceremonial impurity in Israel, requiring isolation from the community. YHWH demonstrates power over both infliction and healing of impurity. The sign's structure — diseased and restored — mirrors the pattern of the Exodus itself: Israel afflicted in Egypt, then restored to health and freedom. Miriam later received the same affliction as divine judgment (Numbers 12:10).
7: The hand restored “like the rest of his flesh” — the instantaneous healing demonstrates that YHWH's power over disease is as complete as his power over creation. The paired signs (staff→serpent, hand diseased→healed) are both reversible: the serpent returns to a staff, the leprous hand is restored. This reversibility distinguishes YHWH's signs from curses: they demonstrate power without permanent harm to Moses, and they anticipate that YHWH can reverse what he afflicts — including the affliction of Egypt.
8: The graduated structure of the three signs — if they don't believe the first, try the second; if not the second, try the third — reflects a rhetorical escalation designed to convince a skeptical audience. The same escalation pattern governs the plague narrative (Exod 7-11): each plague intensifies. YHWH does not demand immediate wholesale belief; he provides cumulative evidence. This pastoral patience in authentication recurs in Gideon's fleece (Judges 6:36-40) and Thomas's need for tactile proof (John 20:27).
9: The third sign — Nile water poured on dry ground becomes blood — is the most portentous: it previews the first plague (Exodus 7:14-25). The Nile was the lifeblood of Egypt, the source of its agricultural abundance and the object of religious veneration (Hapi, the Nile god). Turning its water to blood strikes at Egypt's divine patron and its economic foundation simultaneously. The sign given privately to Moses here is what YHWH will do publicly before all Egypt. The Nile-to-blood connection also echoes the Egyptian infant-killing of Exodus 1:22: Egypt filled the Nile with Israelite blood; now the Nile becomes blood.
10: Moses's objection: “lo ish devarim anochi” — “I am not a man of words.” The irony is significant: Moses was raised in Pharaoh's court, the center of Egyptian literary culture and oratory. Stephen in Acts 7:22 explicitly says Moses was “mighty in words and deeds.” Moses may be voicing genuine psychological paralysis before the task's enormity — the crushing self-doubt that often precedes great service. “Neither in the past nor since you have spoken to your servant” is transparent: speaking with YHWH directly has not removed the speech anxiety. The objection is real, even if YHWH refuses it.
11: YHWH's counter is a sovereignty-of-creation argument: “Who gave human beings their mouths? Who makes them deaf or mute? Who gives them sight or makes them blind? Is it not I, YHWH?” The creator of the faculty can enable it. This is not dismissing Moses's weakness but redirecting his dependence: the question is not whether Moses can speak fluently but whether YHWH, who made the mouth, will enable it. Paul echoes this logic: “I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me” (Philippians 4:13); “power is made perfect in weakness” (2 Corinthians 12:9). The disability listed — deaf, mute, blind — is not accidental: the prophetic mission will encounter all of these, and YHWH is sovereign over each.
12: “Now go; I will be with your mouth and teach you what to say.” Ehyeh im pika — “I will be with your mouth.” The verb ehyeh (I will be) is the same root as the divine name YHWH (Exodus 3:14): the name is an ongoing presence-promise. YHWH's solution to Moses's inability is not a replacement but a promised co-presence. This promise governs all prophetic speech in Israel: “I will put my words in your mouth” (Jeremiah 1:9; Isaiah 51:16; Deuteronomy 18:18). The prophet does not speak of himself but transmits YHWH's word — the human instrument does not need eloquence so long as the divine word is present.
13: Moses's final refusal: “Pardon your servant, Lord. Please send by the hand of whomever you will send.” This is the most direct request for a substitute in Moses's five objections. He has run out of excuses and now simply asks for someone else. YHWH's anger “burned against Moses” (v14) — the only instance of divine anger at Moses in the commissioning narrative — indicates this is no longer humility but resistance. Moses is granted his wish in a modified form: Aaron is sent with him, not instead of him. Divine accommodation meets human weakness without abandoning the divine will.
14: Aaron the Levite — the Levitical connection is emphasized here before the full genealogy of Exodus 6 explains it. Aaron is already “on his way to meet” Moses, suggesting YHWH had already set this in motion before Moses raised the objection. This foreknowledge-and-provision pattern is characteristic: YHWH's response to human complaint is often already prepared. Aaron's speech ability contrasts with Moses's claimed inability — God pairs the weak with the strong, the tongue-tied prophet with the eloquent priest.
15: “You shall speak to him and put words in his mouth; I will be with your mouth and his mouth.” The mediatorial chain: YHWH → Moses → Aaron → people (and Pharaoh). This structure models the prophetic institution: YHWH speaks to the prophet, the prophet speaks to the people. The explicit statement that YHWH will teach both their mouths shows that Aaron's fluency is also divinely enabled, not merely natural talent. Jeremiah 1:9 — “I have put my words in your mouth” — reflects the same model.
16: “He will be as a mouth for you, and you will be as God to him.” This verse defines the prophet-sender relationship with unusual precision: Moses is ke-elohim (like God/gods) to Aaron in the sense that Aaron receives and transmits Moses's words as if they were divine. The same verb is used in 7:1 where YHWH tells Moses he has made him “a god to Pharaoh” with Aaron as prophet. This is not a claim to deity but a description of mediated authority: the one who speaks for God exercises God's authority in the communicative chain. The model explains why attacking the prophet is attacking YHWH (cf. 1 Samuel 8:7).
17: The staff that Moses already holds is now commissioned as the instrument of signs: “take this staff in your hand so you can perform the signs.” The staff passes from ordinary shepherd's tool to “the staff of God” (v20) without any formal ceremony — it is invested with divine purpose by divine appointment. This investiture of ordinary objects for extraordinary purposes recurs throughout Scripture: the ark of the covenant was acacia wood; the temple furnishings were cedar and gold. The holiness is not intrinsic but derived from YHWH's appointment.
18: Moses returns to Jethro his father-in-law and asks permission to return to Egypt: “Let me return to my brothers in Egypt and see whether they are still alive.” The circumspect phrasing hides the mission's true scope. ANE social law required a son-in-law to remain with the wife's family or seek the patriarch's blessing to depart. Moses's return to Jethro is a covenant courtesy as much as a family farewell. Jethro's blessing — “Go in peace” — is not mere politeness but a formal release. Jethro's later role as Moses's father-in-law (Exodus 18) and the Kenite/Midianite connection to Israel's priesthood are debated in biblical scholarship.
19: “All the men who were seeking your life are dead.” The parallel with Matthew 2:20 is striking: the angel's words to Joseph about returning to Israel after Herod's death (“those who sought the child's life are dead”) echo this verse almost verbatim. Matthew's infancy narrative structures Jesus as the new Moses: threatened infancy, escape to Egypt, return when the threat dies. The Exodus pattern is the template for the new Exodus. The Pharaoh under whom Moses committed the killing of the Egyptian (Exodus 2:15) has died — the political situation has changed, opening the way for Moses's return.
20: Moses takes his wife and sons (banav, plural — though the narrative has mentioned only Gershom by name, Exodus 2:22; a second son, Eliezer, appears later in Exodus 18:4), puts them on a donkey, and returns. The donkey is the transport of the ordinary Israelite family; the same image appears at the entry of Jesus into Jerusalem (Zechariah 9:9; Matthew 21:5). “The staff of God” (matteh haelohim) — the ordinary shepherd's rod has been renamed. Moses carries divine authority in his hand.
21: The hardening of Pharaoh's heart — first stated here before Moses has even reached Egypt. The Hebrew verb is ḥizzaqti (I will make strong/harden), from ḥazaq (to be strong, firm). Three Hebrew verbs describe the hardening: ḥazaq (make firm, Exodus 4:21; Exodus 7:13), kaved (make heavy, Exodus 8:15), and qashah (make hard, Exodus 7:3). Pharaoh hardens his own heart (Exodus 7:13, 22; Exodus 8:15, 19, 32; Exodus 9:7) before YHWH hardens it (Exodus 9:12; Exodus 10:1, 20, 27). The hardening serves a revelatory purpose: “that I may multiply my signs.” Paul uses this passage (Romans 9:17-18) to argue divine sovereignty in salvation and judgment — the hardening is not arbitrary but reveals YHWH's character and power before all nations.
22: “Israel is my son, my firstborn” — beni bekhori Yisrael. This is among the most theologically freighted declarations in the Pentateuch. The nation is constituted as YHWH's son, not metaphorically but covenantally. Hosea 11:1 quotes it: “out of Egypt I called my son” — Matthew 2:15 applies this to Jesus, seeing in the return from Egypt a recapitulation of the nation's founding story. The firstborn (bekhor) has special covenant standing and inheritance rights. By declaring Israel his firstborn son, YHWH establishes the theological logic of the tenth plague: Pharaoh's oppression of YHWH's firstborn son is met with the death of Egypt's firstborn sons.
23: “I said to you, let my son go so he may worship me; but you have refused to let him go; therefore I will kill your firstborn son.” The judicial logic is explicit: the penalty mirrors the crime. Pharaoh enslaved YHWH's son; YHWH will execute Pharaoh's son. The lex talionis principle operates at the national level. The firstborn typology is applied in the NT to Christ as YHWH's unique firstborn Son (Luke 1:35; Hebrews 1:5-6; Colossians 1:15, 18), whose death reverses the curse — the true firstborn of God dies so that humanity, enslaved to sin and death, may go free.
24: The bridegroom-of-blood pericope (Exodus 4:24-26) is among the most cryptic passages in the Pentateuch. “At a lodging place on the way, YHWH met him and sought to kill him.” The antecedent of “him” is debated — Moses or his son — but ancient Jewish tradition (and most modern interpreters) read it as Moses. YHWH, who just commissioned Moses, now threatens to kill him. The reason is unstated but resolved by Zipporah's action: Moses had failed to circumcise his son, breaking the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 17:14). The covenant obligation cannot be suspended even for the divinely commissioned deliverer. The blood of circumcision averts divine wrath — a pattern that anticipates the Passover blood's protective function.
25: Zipporah acts with a flint knife (tzor) — the same primitive instrument used for circumcision in Joshua 5:2-3. The urgency and the primitive tool suggest an emergency rite, not a formal ceremony. She “touched his feet” with the foreskin — the idiom for “feet” (raglav) may be a euphemism, or the touching may represent presenting the circumcision blood to Moses as evidence of covenant compliance. “Surely you are a bridegroom of blood to me” — the phrase connects bloodshed with covenant entry. Circumcision is the covenant sign (Genesis 17:11), and entering or remaining in the covenant relationship costs blood. The marital metaphor (chatan, bridegroom) is intentional: the covenant is the marriage; the blood seals it.
26: The parenthetical explanation: “At that time she said ‘bridegroom of blood,’ referring to circumcision.” The Hebrew is terse and the narrator's gloss acknowledges its opacity even to ancient readers. The passage has generated extensive rabbinic and patristic discussion. Its canonical function is to show that even YHWH's chosen servant must be brought under the covenant's blood-demand before he can be the instrument of covenant deliverance. Moses cannot lead Israel in the blood-sign of redemption (the Passover) while his own household lacks the blood-sign of covenant membership (circumcision).
27: Aaron meets Moses at the mountain of God — Horeb/Sinai, the location of the burning bush. YHWH orchestrated the rendezvous before Moses even raised the objection about needing a spokesman (v13-14). The kiss of greeting between brothers (vayishaq lo) is a covenant greeting signifying reconciliation and solidarity. This is their first recorded meeting in Exodus — Moses left Egypt as an infant, raised in Pharaoh's court, then fled to Midian. The reunion of the brothers after decades of separation marks the formation of the leadership pair that will confront Pharaoh together.
28: Moses reports to Aaron “all the words of YHWH who had sent him” and “all the signs he had commanded him to perform.” The accuracy and completeness of prophetic transmission is essential: Moses does not summarize or reinterpret but transmits what he received. This model of prophetic faithfulness — receiving and delivering the exact message — is the standard for all biblical prophecy (Deuteronomy 18:18-20; Jeremiah 26:2). Aaron's role as transmitter of the transmitted word creates a two-stage mediation: YHWH → Moses → Aaron → people.
29: Moses and Aaron gather the elders of Israel. The elders (ziqnei Yisrael) were the tribal and family heads who exercised collective leadership in the absence of a king. Their gathering is the first assembly of Israel's leadership under the new commission — it anticipates the later assembly at Sinai and the council structure of the wilderness period. The elders' role is both political (representing the people) and religious (mediating YHWH's word to the community).
30: Aaron speaks the words and performs the signs before the people. This is exactly the mediation structure YHWH promised: Moses → Aaron (speech) → people (witness). The three signs validated the commission: staff, hand, and water-to-blood (only two signs may have been performed publicly — the water sign was apparently reserved for Egypt itself, cf. Exodus 7:14-25). The signs and words together constitute the full prophetic authentication: speech plus miracle, not miracle alone.
31: The people “believed” (vaya'amen ha'am) and “bowed down and worshipped” (vayishtachavu) when they heard that YHWH had seen their affliction. This corporate faith and worship is the first positive response to YHWH's redemptive initiative in the Exodus narrative. It stands in stark contrast to the cynicism of the overseers (Exodus 5:20-21) and the deafness caused by harsh labor (Exodus 6:9). The people believed not because they saw the miracles only, but because they heard that YHWH was concerned for them (ki paqad YHWH et-beney Yisrael — that YHWH had visited/attended to the children of Israel).
